worth_it_explanation: The IO and LI hierarchy is where most DV360 spend inefficiencies originate. A flat structure with dozens of thin line items prevents automated bidding from gathering enough conversion signal to optimize, and an IO performance goal that contradicts the LI bid strategy beneath it sends the algorithm in two directions at once. These checks read directly inside DV360 and take less time than a single creative revision cycle. The findings from this subcategory inform every downstream bidding and targeting decision.
intro: Display & Video 360 (DV360) organizes spend through a four-level hierarchy: Partner > Advertiser > Campaign > Insertion Order (IO) > Line Item (LI). The IO sets the flight dates, budget, and performance goal for a block of activity. Line items beneath it define how that budget reaches audiences, through which channels, with which bid strategy. When the hierarchy is coherent, the IO goal and the LI bid strategies reinforce each other and automated bidding has a clear signal to optimize toward. When the structure is flat or fragmented, the signal thins out and optimization stalls regardless of budget.

audit_checks.title: IO and line item hierarchy is flat or not organized around a clear campaign goal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A flat structure where one IO holds line items for unrelated goals, channels, or audiences makes budget pacing and performance analysis unreliable. The IO's performance goal applies to the group as a whole, so a mix of upper-funnel display and lower-funnel video line items under one IO produces a blended result that is difficult to read and difficult to act on.

educate.base:
An insertion order (IO) in DV360 is a planning and budget container. It groups line items that share a goal, a flight window, and a budget pool. The IO's performance goal, set in its 'Performance Goal' field, tells DV360 what outcome to optimize toward at the IO level, for example a target cost-per-acquisition (CPA) or a target return on ad spend (ROAS). Line items within it each carry their own bid strategy, but the IO goal sits above them and shapes how budget flows across line items during pacing.

When line items pursuing different goals, for example a reach-oriented video LI and a conversion-oriented display LI, share one IO, the IO-level performance goal cannot be meaningful for both. The pacing system tries to hit one number for the whole IO, and the individual LIs fight for budget according to a goal that fits neither of them well. The result is uneven pacing, muddied reporting, and a performance goal metric in the IO summary that blends incompatible signals.

A well-structured account groups line items by goal first, then by channel or format. A prospecting IO holds upper-funnel line items. A retargeting IO holds conversion-focused line items. Each group gets an IO-level goal that describes the actual outcome the group is working toward.

A few signs point to a flat or misaligned hierarchy:
  • One IO contains both awareness and conversion line items running simultaneously
  • The IO performance goal is set to 'None' or to a metric that applies only to a subset of its line items
  • Multiple IOs share a campaign but their goals overlap with no clear differentiation by funnel stage or channel
You may want to map each IO's stated performance goal against the bid strategies of its line items to see whether the two levels are pointing toward the same outcome.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, prospecting line items targeting new audiences and retargeting line items targeting cart abandoners serve different conversion windows and cost-per-result expectations. Grouping them under one IO with a single target CPA produces a blended number that undervalues the prospecting work and overstates the retargeting efficiency. You may want to consider separating them into distinct IOs with goals that reflect the intent of each group.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, an IO that mixes brand awareness video with a lead-form display campaign makes the IO-level cost-per-lead metric uninterpretable. The video LIs contribute impressions, not leads, so the IO's CPA reads high even when the display LIs are performing well. You may want to consider structuring IOs around a single conversion goal so the IO-level metric reflects what the group is actually optimizing toward.

learn_more.source_title: About insertion orders in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: Open the campaign in DV360 and review the IO list, the goal each IO carries, and the line items nested within each IO.

dv360_ui.label: Display & Video 360 UI
steps: Open the campaign and select the 'Insertion Orders' tab. For each IO, note the performance goal type and value shown in the 'Performance Goal' column.
Open each IO and review the line items it contains. Note the bid strategy on each LI, the format type (display, video, audio, connected TV (CTV)), and the audience targeting applied.
For each IO, ask whether the LIs beneath it share the same conversion goal or funnel intent. An IO whose LIs span awareness and conversion objectives is a candidate for restructuring.
Note any IO with 'Performance Goal' set to 'None' or to a metric such as viewability percentage when the majority of its LIs are running toward a conversion goal.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per IO, the performance goal type and the goal types of the line items beneath it. An IO where all LIs share the same funnel intent and the IO goal matches that intent passes. An IO where LIs pursue different goals, or where the IO performance goal is mismatched to the LI bid strategies, is a fail recorded with the IO name and the conflicting LI names.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IO name, its performance goal, and the bid strategies of its line items above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the insertion order hierarchy in {{site_name}}'s DV360 account mixes line items with different funnel goals under single IOs. When awareness and conversion line items share one IO, the IO-level performance goal cannot guide pacing toward a coherent outcome for either group, and reporting at the IO level blends signals that reflect different stages of the funnel. {{voice_recommend}} restructuring the affected IOs so that each one holds line items pursuing the same goal, then setting the IO performance goal to match, so that the pacing system and the IO-level metrics both reflect a single, interpretable outcome.

audit_checks.title: Line items are split too thin for automated bidding to gather sufficient signal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: DV360's automated bidding learns from conversion signal at the line item level. A line item that receives fewer than roughly 50 conversions per week does not give the algorithm enough data to set bids accurately, so it falls back to under- or over-bidding. Splitting budget across many thin line items is one of the most direct ways to degrade automated bidding performance without changing any creative or targeting.

educate.base:
DV360 automated bidding, including 'Maximize conversions' and target cost-per-action (CPA) strategies, sets bids at auction time using a model it builds from the conversion history of each line item. The model updates continuously, but it needs a minimum volume of conversions within a rolling window to make reliable predictions. Google's guidance for comparable automated bidding systems recommends at least 50 conversions per week at the line item level before the strategy is fully optimized. Below that threshold, the model bids on thin data and the actual CPA drifts from the target.

Over-fragmentation happens when practitioners split line items by dimensions that do not need to be separate: one LI per creative size, one LI per day part, one LI per narrow audience segment. Each split divides the conversion pool further. A campaign with 200 conversions per week spread across 20 line items averages 10 conversions per LI, which is well below the threshold at which automated bidding stabilizes.

The cost of fragmentation is not always visible in the LI's status. A line item can show 'Active' and 'Delivering' while bidding on a signal too thin to produce a reliable CPA. The symptom appears later, in a target CPA that the line item consistently misses or in volatile cost-per-result from week to week.

A few signs point to over-fragmentation:
  • More than five or six line items under one IO pursuing the same goal against similar audiences
  • Individual line items converting fewer than 50 times per week while running a conversion-based bid strategy
  • Line items split by creative dimension, day part, or minor audience variation rather than by a meaningful strategic difference
You may want to consider consolidating line items that differ only by creative size or minor audience variation into fewer, broader LIs so that the conversion signal concentrates.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A retailer running separate line items for each product category with a target CPA strategy faces a fragmentation problem when no single category generates enough weekly purchases to stabilize the model. You may want to consider consolidating product categories into fewer line items and using audience signals or custom bidding adjustments within a single LI to differentiate performance by category without splitting the conversion pool.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation advertiser that splits line items by form type, geographic region, and audience simultaneously may have each LI receiving only a handful of leads per week. You may want to consider consolidating the geographic and form-type dimensions into a single LI and allowing the automated bidder to optimize across the full lead pool, using campaign-level or IO-level reporting to track the dimensional breakdown.

learn_more.source_title: About automated bidding in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: Review each IO's line item count, the weekly conversion volume per LI, and the bid strategy each LI uses.

dv360_ui.label: Display & Video 360 UI
steps: Open the campaign and select 'Line Items'. Filter to the date range of the past four weeks.
For each line item using a conversion-based bid strategy (e.g., 'Maximize conversions' or a target CPA), note the total conversions in that window. Divide by four to estimate the weekly average.
Flag any line item averaging fewer than 50 conversions per week while running an automated conversion bid strategy.
Count the total line items under each IO. An IO with more than five or six similar line items targeting the same goal is a candidate to consolidate.
Note whether line items within the same IO differ by a meaningful strategic dimension (e.g., prospecting vs. retargeting, display vs. CTV) or by a minor variation (e.g., one LI per creative size).

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line item running an automated conversion bid strategy, the weekly conversion average over the past four weeks. A line item averaging 50 or more conversions per week passes. A line item averaging fewer than 50 conversions per week is a fail recorded with the LI name, the weekly average, and the bid strategy in use.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the line item name, bid strategy, and four-week weekly conversion average above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several line items in {{site_name}}'s DV360 account are running automated conversion bid strategies with conversion volumes too low for the algorithm to optimize reliably. When a line item receives fewer than 50 conversions per week, the bidding model works from insufficient data and the actual cost per result drifts from the target. The root cause is a structure that splits conversion signal across too many line items. {{voice_recommend}} consolidating line items that differ only by creative dimension or minor audience variation into fewer, broader line items so that the weekly conversion volume per LI reaches the threshold at which automated bidding can optimize accurately.

audit_checks.title: IO performance goal does not align with the bid strategies of the line items beneath it
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the IO performance goal measures one outcome and the line items beneath it bid toward a different outcome, the IO-level optimization and reporting work from conflicting signals. Budget pacing at the IO level responds to the IO goal, which may accelerate or slow spending on line items that are actually performing well by their own bid strategy's metric.

educate.base:
In Display & Video 360 (DV360), the insertion order (IO) and each of its line items (LIs) carry separate goal and strategy fields. The IO's 'Performance Goal' type and value, for example a target cost-per-mille (CPM), a target CPA, or a target ROAS, describes what the IO is trying to achieve as a whole. Each LI underneath it carries its own bid strategy, for example 'Maximize clicks', 'Maximize conversions', or a fixed CPM, which governs how that LI bids at each auction.

DV360 uses the IO performance goal in several ways. It informs the pacing algorithm, which adjusts how quickly the IO spends its budget to stay on track toward the goal. It also appears in the IO summary reporting as the headline performance metric. When the IO goal and the LI bid strategies describe different outcomes, the pacing algorithm optimizes toward a metric the line items are not bidding toward, and the IO summary metric does not reflect the actual optimization objective.

A straightforward example: an IO with a performance goal of 'viewability percentage' contains line items bidding on target CPA. The pacing system monitors viewability rate at the IO level, but the LIs are competing in auctions based on predicted conversions. The IO-level metric and the LI-level bidding are measuring different things, and neither one informs the other correctly.

A few signs point to a goal-strategy mismatch:
  • The IO performance goal type is a brand metric (viewability, reach, or completion rate) while its line items run conversion-based bid strategies
  • The IO performance goal is set to a target CPA but the line items use fixed CPM or maximize clicks strategies
  • The IO performance goal shows 'None' on an IO whose line items are running automated bid strategies that depend on a stated goal
You may want to confirm that the IO performance goal type matches the bid strategy category of the majority of its line items before assuming the IO-level reporting reflects the actual optimization intent.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: An ecommerce IO with a performance goal of target ROAS but line items set to 'Maximize clicks' is not sending return on ad spend (ROAS) signal to the bidding algorithm. The LIs will bid to get the most clicks within the budget, which is not the same as bidding to maximize purchase revenue. You may want to confirm that LIs using revenue-focused bid strategies report Floodlight conversion values so that any ROAS-based goal at the IO level reflects real transaction data.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation IO with a target CPA goal but display line items using a fixed CPM strategy will pace based on cost-per-lead targets while actually paying a fixed price per thousand impressions regardless of conversion performance. You may want to confirm whether the IO goal is intended to guide pacing or to report post-view performance, and adjust the LI bid strategies to match so the two levels describe the same optimization objective.

learn_more.source_title: Set performance goals for insertion orders
About automated bidding in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: For each IO, confirm the performance goal type and compare it with the bid strategy category of the line items beneath it.

dv360_ui.label: Display & Video 360 UI
steps: Open the campaign and select the 'Insertion Orders' tab. Note the performance goal type for each IO, for example 'Target CPA', 'Target viewability', 'Maximize conversions', or 'None'.
Open each IO and view its line items. For each LI, note the bid strategy type shown in the 'Bid Strategy' column.

Compare the IO goal category with the LI bid strategy categories. An IO targeting a conversion-based goal should have LIs running conversion-based bid strategies. An IO targeting a brand metric should have LIs bidding on reach, viewability, or completion rate.

Flag any IO where the goal type is a brand metric but the majority of LIs run conversion-based strategies, or vice versa. Also flag any IO with 'Performance Goal: None' whose LIs use automated bid strategies that require a stated goal to function.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per IO, the performance goal type and the bid strategy types of its line items. An IO where the performance goal category matches the bid strategy category of its line items passes (e.g., conversion-goal IO with conversion-bid LIs; brand-goal IO with CPM or viewability-bid LIs). An IO where the goal and the LI strategies describe different outcome categories is a fail recorded with the IO name, the goal type, and the conflicting LI bid strategy types.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IO name, performance goal type, and line item bid strategy types above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more insertion orders in {{site_name}}'s DV360 account carry a performance goal that does not match the bid strategies of the line items beneath them. When the IO goal and the LI bid strategies describe different outcomes, the IO-level pacing works from a metric the line items are not bidding toward, and the IO summary reporting does not reflect the actual optimization objective. {{voice_recommend}} aligning the IO performance goal type with the bid strategy category of its line items so that the pacing, the reporting, and the LI bidding all measure the same outcome.
